ata-driven approaches are becoming increasingly preva-
ent for modeling, predicting, controlling, and designing com-
lex nonlinear dynamical systems across a variety of scien-
ific and technical fields [7]. Data-driven approaches are es-
ecially effective for analyzing experimental observations of
omplex dynamical systems for which no accurate equations
re available, or analyses for which knowledge of empiri-
al or first-principles governing nonlinear equations must be
nterrogated in order to understand the system’s behavior.
uantitative comparisons (dissimilarity measures) between
onlinear dynamical systems based on data are particularly
mportant for a variety of machine learning and optimiza-
ion tasks. As a result,  it is desirable for data-driven dis-
imilarity measures to (a) be computationally efficient such
hat the approach is scalable to analyzing multiple combi-
ations of systems [39,14] and (b) respect the inherent fea-
ures of the dynamical system that generated the data [31].
he first attribute implies an ‘efficient’ quantitative measure
here ‘efficient’ is widely accepted to mean it can be com-
uted in polynomial time or better [5]. The second desired
ttribute requires that the approach be anchored to an appro-
riate equivalence relationship for nonlinear dynamical sys-
ems so that systems that are equivalent return zero dissim-
larity when compared. Topological conjugacy [37], which
rovides conditions on both trajectories and operator level
issimilarity, is the equivalence relationship explored here.
There has been extensive work in comparing linear dy-
amical systems from both a trajectory geometry and op-
rator perspective. Efficient kernel based approaches were
eveloped in [35,2]. These approaches quantify dissimilar-

2
ity based on trajectory geometry and follow the behavioral  s
framework perspective to identifying dynamical systems [38].  c
While the focus was on linear dynamical systems, an ex-  a
tension to nonlinear systems was briefly discussed in [35].  t
The nonlinear systems formulation from [35] closely resem-  c
bles a Koopman operator [8] perspective. However, system  b
or operator theoretic dissimilarity measures were not con-  p
sidered. A systems oriented perspective to computing ‘dis-  w
tance’ between linear dynamical systems was later taken  r
in [1,14]. It was recognized that restricting to the orthog-  i
onal (maximally compact) subgroup of real invertible ma-  e
trices could reduce the computational expense of solving  w
for optimal alignments between systems. Topological con-  t
jugacy considerations were not explored in [1,14]. Further-  m
more, generalizations to complex transformations and the  e
relevance to dynamical systems was not studied.  2
Another trajectory focused approach is generalized shape  w
metrics [39], which focused on scalability and computational  l
efficiency. It was also shown how generalized shape metrics  t
relate to (and are often equivalent to) other statistical shape  t
approaches such as canonical correlation analysis [12] and  p
representational similarity analysis [16]. Similar to [1,14],  a
the approach is based on the assumption of orthogonal trans-  a
formations between data sets. By restricting to orthogonal  q
transformations it was shown in [39] that the resulting pseu-  m
dometrics for comparing systems satisfy subadditivity (i.e.  v
triange inequality) [36]. This was considered an important
feature for large scale machine learning applications where  o
inability to satisfy the triangle inequality can lead to inconsi-  w
tent results. Furthermore, the alignment transformations are  r
solved using the orthogonal one-sided Procrustes solution  r
[30] which can be computed in polynomial (cubic) time.  t
However, the generalized shape metric approach does not  s
account for the inherent properties of the underlying dynam-  c
ical system operator.  n
Beginning from the perspective that topological conju-  f
gacy is the foremost equivalence relationship in dynamical  p
systems, the homeomorphic defect approach to comparing  l
nearly conjugate dynamical systems was introduced in [31].  o
The homeomorphic defect approach assumes one has found  w
a transformation between two systems such that the trajecto-  [
ries match. This transformation function was referred to as a  O
‘commuter.’ If the commuter function is a homeomorphism,  c
then the systems are topologically conjugate. Therefore, dis-  i
similarity was quantified according to the ‘defects’ in the  t
commuter function that prevented it from being a homeo-
morphism. Scalable computation of the commuter functions  d
from a data-driven context, as well as quantifying dissimi-  o
larity when a commuter function cannot be found were not  c
explored.

T
Koopman operator theory provides an attractive avenue  a
for data-driven dissimilarity quantification between nonlin-  m
ear dynamical systems since it has been shown to be well  m

Bryan Glaz
uited to capturing topological conjugacy [8,22]. Theoreti-
al development of pseudometrics based on Koopman oper-
tors was first explored in [23] and [21] where it was shown
hat harmonic averages over a single observable function
omposed with a complete set of periodic functions could
e used to quantify dissimilarity between systems. This ap-
roach is especially well suited to experimental situations in
hich one may have access to limited measurements. More
ecently, Koopman operator level attributes were considered
n [28,27] where the Wasserstein distance on the Koopman
igenvalues was used to quantify dissimilarity. This approach
as theoretically based on the insight that if two systems are
opologically conjugate, then they will have the same Koop-
an eigenvalues and their Koopman eigenfunctions will be
quivalent under transformation [8]. The approach in [28,
7] was effective at identifying equivalence from data even
hen two systems are topologically conjugate under a non-
inear transformation. Applicability for comparisons of sys-
ems with unequal dimensionality was also discussed. Fur-
hermore, computing the Wasserstein distance is solvable in
olynomial time since it only requires solution to the linear
ssignment problem for a permutation matrix that optimally
ligns the Koopman eigenvalues between two systems. The
uestion of how optimal alignment from a Wasserstein per-
utation perspective affects trajectory dissimilarity (and vice
ersa) remains open, particularly away from conjugacy.
Koopman operators served as the foundation for devel-
pment of dynamic similarity analysis (DSA)[24], which
as introduced as an extension to generalized shape met-
ics. The focus of DSA was on accounting for system level,
ather than trajectory level, dissimilarity while maintaining
he advantages of upholding the triangle inequality. As a re-
ult, the analysis of [39] was adapted to the two-sided Pro-
rustes problem in [24] where it was shown that orthogo-
al (or special orthogonal) transformations guarantee satis-
action of the triangle inequality. However, because the ap-
roach requires solution to a non-convex optimization prob-
em, it is not solvable in polynomial time even if using fast
ptimization algorithms such as the one described in [14]. It
as shown in [24] that DSA recovers the pseudometric from
28,27] if the data-driven Koopman operators are normal.
therwise, as in [1,14], DSA cannot be guaranteed to re-
over zero dissimilarity for topologically conjugate systems
n general due to the assumption of orthogonal transforma-
ions in observable space.
Therefore the main contributions presented here are to
evelop computationally efficient pseudometrics that are the-
retically consistent with topological conjugacy while ac-
ounting for implications of being away from conjugacy.
he approach is based on data-driven Koopman operators
nd accounts for both operator and trajectory dissimilarity
easures within a unified framework. This is done by for-
ulating deviation from conjugacy pseudometrics based on.

The conceptual foundation for comparing/classifying sys-  t
tems is that pseudometrics should return zero when two sys-  s
tems are equivalent. For dynamical systems, topological con-  e
jugacy serves as the theoretical foundation for equivalence  m
[37]. If two dynamical systems are topologically conjugate  9
to another, then they are equivalent. Building off the notion  s
of equivalence, one can quantify dissimilarity between dy-  f
namical systems by their deviation from conjugacy, i.e. if  S
the deviation from conjugacy between system a and b is less  v
than the deviation between system b and c, then among sys-  b
tems a and c, we can quantitatively say that a is more sim-
ilar to b. A brief description of topological conjugacy and  P
the connection with Koopman based data-driven approaches  t

As an engineering example, morphological computation [25]
of a single degree of freedom mechanical actuator driven by  a
a controller to achieve stable hopping is considered. Mor-  d
phological computation is a measure of how much the body  n

require less information to be processed by a control sys-  t

low morphological computation. In particular, actuation and  r

motor actuators are compared [9,11].

because the dynamics were shown to provide more insight  c
than time averaged quantities. Since morphological com-  i
putation is a nonlinear state-dependent quantity it can be
treated as a Koopman observable.  Ψ

Computationally efficient solutions for pseudometrics quan-
tifying deviation from topological conjugacy between dy-

Bryan Glaz
amical systems were presented. The theoretical justifica-
ion for computing such pseudometrics in Koopman eigen-
unction space rather than observable space was provided.
his simiplification, along with Pareto optimality consid-
rations led to the conclusion that optimal transformations
or comparing systems could be obtained from the group of
nitary matrices. It was shown that the pseudometrics could
e computed in polynomial time. The resulting practical ad-
antages in terms of efficiency, scalability, and theoretical
onsistency were discussed.
Results demonstrated the effectiveness of the deviation
rom conjugacy pseudometrics. A simple benchmarking prob-
em was used to show that deviation from conjugacy re-
over a value of zero at topological conjugacy even when the
nderlying transformation between systems is non-unitary.
urthermore, the benefit of considering a Pareto optimal con-
ext for deviation from conjugacy when trajectory geome-
ry is important was illustrated by showing that considering
pectral operator error alone may lead to conclusions on dis-
imilarity that are not reflected when comparing trajectories.
n contrast, the deviation from conjugacy pseudometrics ac-
omodate both measures of dissimilarity in a consistent for-
alism and avoid such pitfalls. Finally, an engineering ex-
mple was presented in which biological nonlinear muscle
ased mechanical actuation systems were compared to DC
otor and linearized muscle systems. The pseudometrics
ere able to quantify dissimilary between the systems based
n the dynamics of morphological computation (a Koopman
bservable). It was also shown that while the DC motor and
inearizedmuscle have relatively similar time-averagedmor-
hological computation, they do not have similar morpho-
ogical computation dynamics.
